mnemonics: MAP I/O: Memory-mapped I/O vs. Port-mapped I/O. Memory-mapped treats I/O devices as memory locations, while Port-mapped uses special I/O instructions.
ISR Steps: ISR (Interrupt Service Routine) steps: Save context, Service interrupt, Restore context, Return (SSRR).
DMA Does: DMA (Direct Memory Access) does: Data transfer without CPU, freeing it for other tasks.
keyPoints: Memory-mapped I/O uses the same address space for memory and devices, simplifying instructions but consuming memory addresses. Port-mapped I/O uses a separate address space and special instructions (e.g., IN/OUT).
Programmed I/O (polling) has the CPU constantly check device status, which is simple but inefficient. Interrupt-driven I/O allows the device to signal the CPU when ready, improving efficiency.
DMA controllers handle bulk data transfers (e.g., disk I/O) directly between device and memory without CPU intervention, significantly improving performance for large data moves.
commonMistakes: Confusing Memory-mapped I/O with Port-mapped I/O: forgetting that memory-mapped uses memory instructions (e.g., LOAD/STORE) while port-mapped requires specific I/O instructions (e.g., IN/OUT).
Assuming polling is always worse: while interrupt-driven I/O is generally more efficient, polling can be preferable for simple systems or when response time must be guaranteed and predictable.

Overlooking the need for synchronization: forgetting that with DMA, the CPU and DMA controller must coordinate to avoid accessing the same memory location simultaneously, requiring mechanisms like bus arbitration.
